turns.text: Hello Katelin, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s fine, I can talk. Though, to be honest, it’s strange to be on this side of the microphone. I’ve spent so much time on the other side—reading the journals, analyzing the datasets, thinking I was prepared because I knew the vocabulary. But knowing the pathophysiology of pre-eclampsia doesn’t stop your hands from shaking when the blood pressure cuff keeps coming back with those jagged, dangerous numbers.

It was lonely. That’s the only way to describe it. I’d be in the exam room, and I could see them reading my chart, making quick assessments, and then switching to this tone of voice they use for—well, for someone like me. A young, single mom. They’d simplify the explanations, and I’d have to bite my tongue to keep from correcting their terminology. I didn’t need a simplified version; I needed someone to look at me and acknowledge that I knew exactly how thin the ice was beneath us. 

Now that I’m home, the silence is almost worse. I look at my baby and I see a "survival statistic." It’s an exhausting way to love someone. I’m just trying to figure out how to be a mother and not just a patient who survived a high-risk pregnancy. Where do you even start with that?
Tell me about the first days and weeks after the birth — what that time was like for you, physically and emotionally.

Those first few weeks were… a blur of survival mode, honestly. Coming home felt less like a celebration and more like I’d just stepped off a ledge. Every time the baby slept, my brain didn’t just rest; it cycled through worst-case scenarios. I’d be sitting in my living room, watching the monitors or just staring at the baby’s chest rise and fall, and I’d catch myself checking for signs of respiratory distress because that was the "data" I spent years documenting. 

Physically, I felt betrayed by my own body. After dealing with the hypertension, the postpartum period was just another mountain to climb. I felt so depleted, like my nerves were permanently frayed. And the loneliness… it hits different when you’re home alone. I’d have these moments where I wanted to call my doctor to ask something specific about my recovery, but then I’d stop. I knew they’d just give me the standard, "You’re doing great, mom," line, and I didn't want to be "mom"—I wanted to be a person who was terrified and needed someone to actually listen to my concerns without treating me like a child. 

I think the hardest part was realizing that my medical team was done with me the second I was discharged. I was no longer a "case" to solve, just a young woman left to figure out the aftermath on my own.
Tell me about feeding your baby — how you made decisions about it, and what the experience was actually like.

Feeding was… complicated. It felt like another arena where I couldn't just be a mom—I had to be a researcher again. I remember staring at the baby, trying to weigh the biological benefits against the sheer mental exhaustion I was carrying. Everything felt like a calculated risk, which is a miserable way to approach something that’s supposed to be natural and bonding.

I felt a lot of pressure, mostly from myself. I knew the literature on neonatal immunity, and that made me feel like if I didn't push through every struggle, I was failing the baby on a cellular level. But my body was still reeling from the pre-eclampsia, and the stress was—well, it wasn't exactly helping with supply. It was this constant, quiet battle. 

I’d be up at 3:00 a.m. in the dark, feeding and feeling like I was under a microscope. I’d try to ask questions during appointments, but the providers would just give me those practiced, reassuring smiles, like they were patting my hand. They didn’t get that I wasn't asking because I was clueless; I was asking because I was looking for some agency in a situation where I’d felt so out of control. It wasn't just about milk; it was about reclaiming some power, and honestly, that’s just really hard to explain to someone who thinks you’re just another "young, single mom."
Tell me about how you were feeling emotionally in the weeks and months after the birth — the full picture, not just the positive side.

It feels like I’m living in two different worlds at the same time. On one hand, there’s the sheer, bone-deep exhaustion of being a single mother of two. You have this tiny, fragile person who depends on you for every single breath, and you’re doing it on almost no sleep. But the "full picture"? It’s haunting. 

I’m constantly fighting this internal battle between my heart and my head. My head is still working like a researcher—calculating risks, watching for symptoms, and remembering the trauma of those monitors beeping in the hospital. My heart wants to just be present, to just look at my baby and feel joy without that layer of clinical dread coating everything. People tell me, "Oh, you're so lucky, everything turned out fine," and I want to scream because they don't see the anxiety that keeps me awake when the house is quiet. 

I feel like a ghost in my own life sometimes. I’m the woman who knows the "why" and "how" of the complications I faced, but that knowledge just makes me feel more isolated. I’m not just a mom; I’m a mom who’s waiting for the other shoe to drop, and it’s exhausting to carry that weight while everyone else assumes I’ve just "moved on." I haven't. I'm still right there, back in that hospital bed, terrified.
Tell me about your physical recovery after the birth — what that process was like and what support you had.

Physical recovery was this bizarre, hollow experience. The hospital gave me the pamphlets—the standard discharge instructions that tell you what to look for and when to call—but those papers felt so disconnected from the actual reality of my body. My blood pressure remained the primary focus for everyone; as long as the numbers weren't hitting a crisis point, I was essentially "recovered" in their eyes. 

But my body didn't feel like it belonged to me. I felt like a vessel that had been put through a wringer. Between the physical trauma and the emotional weight of it all, my nerves felt fried. And support? That’s a delicate subject. Being a single mother means the "support" is often just me trying to be invisible while I handle the pain, because I’m so focused on making sure the kids are okay. 

I didn't really have anyone who understood the intersection of the medical trauma and the physical healing. When I mentioned the lingering fatigue or the way my body felt foreign, the providers just treated it as "normal postpartum adjustment." It’s frustrating. They were measuring my recovery against a chart, but they weren't measuring it against the person I actually am. I felt like I was recovering in a vacuum.
Tell me about how your sense of yourself changed after you became a parent — your identity, your relationships, your daily life.

My identity feels like it was dismantled and put back together in a way that doesn't quite fit the person I used to be. Before, I was "Katelin the researcher," the one who looked at data as a way to control the future. Now? I’m someone who knows that control is an illusion. That’s a heavy thing to carry at twenty-three. It’s like I’ve lost my innocence about how "safe" the world really is. 

My relationships feel different, too. I don't really know how to talk to people who haven't been through this, because if I say, "I'm worried about X, Y, or Z," they try to "fix" me with optimism. They don't understand that I'm not looking for a silver lining; I’m looking for validation that the trauma I lived through was real. It’s hard to relate to friends my age who are still just navigating their twenties, while I’m navigating long-term cardiovascular risks and the weight of being a single mother of two. 

Daily life is just a constant negotiation. I’m always balancing the survival of my children with the need to keep myself afloat, and the "researcher" in me is always lurking, analyzing every cry and every sleep cycle. I miss the person I was before the blood pressure started to climb—the one who thought she could outsmart the system. Now, I’m just trying to figure out who I am when the clinical data isn't the only thing that matters.
Were there parts of your previous self — your work identity, social life, sense of independence — that felt hard to hold onto?

It feels like I’m mourning a version of myself that died somewhere between my first high-risk reading and the delivery. That professional part of me—the one who spent hours buried in journals, analyzing those neonatal outcomes with a sense of clinical detachment—feels almost unreachable now. It’s infuriating, really. I spent so much time being the person who provided answers, and now I’m the person who has to ask the questions, and the answers I get are never enough.

My independence has been the hardest thing to hold onto. Being a single mother, there is no "off-switch." You can't just be a student or a researcher when your baby is screaming or your blood pressure is spiking. You have to be the parent, the patient, and the advocate all at once. And my social life? It’s essentially non-existent. How do you go out for drinks or talk about career moves with people who think "high risk" is just a phrase you hear in a movie? I feel like I’m speaking a different language than my peers. I’m living in a world of potential complications and long-term health monitoring, and they’re living in a world of brunch and casual dating. The gap between us feels like a canyon, and it’s just getting wider every day.
Tell me about returning to work — or thinking about returning to work — and what that transition was like for you.

Returning to work feels like stepping back into a room where the air is different, and I’m not sure I can breathe it anymore. When I think about it, I don't just feel the typical "mom guilt" about leaving the kids. It’s deeper. That professional identity—the one I built on logic and cold, hard data—feels like a costume that doesn't fit right anymore. How am I supposed to sit there and contribute to research on perinatal outcomes knowing exactly how it feels to be the outlier in the spreadsheet?

There’s a part of me that wants to hide the fact that I was a high-risk patient. In my field, there’s an unspoken pressure to keep the personal and professional separated. But how do I unsee what I’ve lived? If I go back, I feel like I’ll be sitting in meetings, hearing my colleagues talk about "patient outcomes," and I’ll have to force myself not to stand up and scream, "You're missing the person behind the graph!" 

The financial reality of being a single mother is the only thing forcing my hand, really. I need the stability, but I’m terrified that by going back, I’ll be forced to compartmentalize my own trauma just to function. I’m not just a researcher; I’m a case study now, and I’m not sure I’m ready to sit on that side of the desk again.
How did you feel about going back — was it something you looked forward to, something you dreaded, or both?

It’s both, which makes me feel incredibly guilty. Part of me—the part that used to find comfort in clinical structure and intellectual stimulation—is starving for it. I want to be back in an environment where I’m valued for my brain, not just for my utility as a mother or my status as a "high-risk patient." I want to feel capable again, like I’m not just defined by my blood pressure readings and the fear that follows me home every day.

But there’s a much louder part of me that is just… terrified. I dread the idea of having to perform "normalcy." I know that if I go back, I’ll be expected to just pick up where I left off, to be that same sharp, objective researcher. But I can’t be her anymore. Every time I read a study now, I’m not just looking for the P-value; I’m looking for the human error, the missed symptoms, the moments of dismissal I experienced myself. 

The idea of navigating the workplace—where I’ll have to constantly toggle between my trauma and my professional output—is exhausting. I’m scared that if I go back, I’ll either break down or realize that I’ve fundamentally outgrown the life I worked so hard to build. It’s like trying to return to a house that burned down while you were away. You can go back, but the foundation just isn’t what it used to be.
Tell me about how you used digital tools, apps, forums, or online communities after the birth — what role they played in your life as a new parent.

I have a love-hate relationship with the digital stuff. At first, I was constantly on those pregnancy and postpartum forums, but I’d find myself analyzing the "data" being shared by other moms. I’d see someone post about their blood pressure or their recovery and I’d catch myself critiquing their doctors' care plans, or getting triggered by stories that echoed my own. It felt like I was back at my desk doing a literature review, only this time the subject matter was my own life. It wasn't exactly relaxing.

There’s this pressure in those apps to hit all the milestones, to log every ounce of milk and every hour of sleep. It feels like you're being graded on your motherhood. I actually deleted most of them eventually. They made the "researcher" in me spin out because I started comparing my own recovery to an aggregate of everyone else’s, and that just made me feel even more isolated. 

When I did reach out online, it was mostly to find someone who wasn't just "happy and overwhelmed." I needed to find people who understood that clinical dread isn't something you can just "meditate" away. It was a mixed bag, though. Sometimes it helped to vent, but other times, the sheer volume of medical anxiety in those spaces just fueled my own. It was like living in an echo chamber of my worst fears.
Tell me about how you would describe your overall experience of maternity care — from preconception through to now. What would you want future services to know?

If I had to describe it in one word, it would be "erasure." From the moment I was labeled "high-risk," it felt like I was being slowly erased. My history, my own clinical knowledge, and my personhood were all pushed aside to make room for the data they needed to collect. I wasn’t a woman having a baby; I was a pathology that needed to be managed, and once the baby was out, I was considered "resolved."

If I could tell future services anything, it’s this: stop treating us like textbooks. When you simplify your language, you aren't being helpful—you’re being condescending. It isolates us. I needed a provider who would sit with me in the discomfort of that "clinical dread" instead of just throwing me a generic brochure or a pat on the shoulder. 

Also, understand that the trauma doesn't stop at discharge. The physical care is only half the battle; the emotional "aftermath" is where you really lose people. We need to be seen as human beings for more than just the duration of our pregnancy. Please, stop looking at the chart and start looking at the person in the chair. You might be surprised by how much we actually understand, and how much we really need you to just listen.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that gets lost—even in these kinds of conversations—is the sheer, quiet indignity of being a "young, unmarried woman" in a clinical setting. It’s a subtle, constant thing. It’s the way a nurse might talk to me like I’m a child who doesn't understand the risks, or the way a doctor ignores my questions because they’ve already decided I’m just "anxious" rather than informed. 

That bias, whether it’s implicit or explicit, changes the quality of care. It makes you feel like you have to perform a certain kind of compliance just to be treated with basic respect. I wasn't just fighting pre-eclampsia; I was fighting the perception of who I was supposed to be as a single mother. That added such a heavy, unnecessary layer of exhaustion to my pregnancy and recovery.

If future care models want to truly be "innovative," they need to address that bias. Don't just give me the medical stats; give me the space to be a person, an intellect, and a mother all at once. Treat me like the partner in my own care that I actually am. I’m not a liability to be managed; I’m a human being who was just trying to survive, and I deserved to be treated as a whole person from the start. That's all. Thank you for listening—I really mean that.
